title: On Extending Direct Preference Optimization to Accommodate Ties
abstract: We derive and investigate two DPO variants that explicitly model the possibility of declaring a tie in pair-wise comparisons. We replace the Bradley-Terry model in DPO with two well-known modeling extensions, by Rao and Kupper and by Davidson, that assign probability to ties as alternatives to clear preferences. Our experiments in neural machine translation and summarization show that explicitly labeled ties can be added to the datasets for these DPO variants without  the degradation in task performance that is observed when the same tied pairs are presented to DPO. We find empirically that the inclusion of ties leads to stronger regularization with respect to the reference policy as measured by KL divergence, and we see this even for DPO in its original form. We provide a theoretical explanation for this regularization effect using ideal DPO policy theory. We further show performance improvements over DPO in translation and mathematical reasoning using our DPO variants. We find it can be beneficial to include ties in preference optimization rather than simply discard them, as is done in common practice.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In this paper, the authors extend DPO with the ability to model ties (i.e., cases where the winner and loser are equally good or comparable). As the authors convincingly argue, their approach is a more principled alternative to simply removing such cases, which is a common pre-processing step in DPO due to known empirical disadvantages of having tied data instances. (though not mentioned, ties, or what are called "indifference relations" ~ are a well study theme in the broader literature on preference, see [Hansson, Sven Ove and Till Grüne-Yanoff, 2024]. The authors might consider citing this work as additional motivation). 

To derive their variant of DPO, they rely on two established variants of Bradley-Terry (BT) that accomodate ties (named R(ao)K(upper) and D(avidson)). Both variants and very nicely summarized in Sec. 2.2 and (loosely speaking) define a distribution over w > l, l > w and a tie w ~ l via additional parameters (the difference in the two parameterizations of RK and D are discussed and explained from first principles in lines 74-87). They then use these two extensions to straightforwardly derive a general DPO variant (Eq. 14), one that factors into two loss terms that compete against one another, a term for clear winners with a binary weight and a term for ties with the same binary weight (both parameterized by either RK or D, giving rise to DPO-RK and DPO-D). Importantly, this general approach requires having an annotated set of tie and non-tie instances. (additional gradient analysis is provided in 2.3.1 that helps give intuition for how this differs from vanilla DPO). 

Experiments are performed for machine translation, text summarization and math reasoning. Ties and non-ties are obtained heuristically using outside models to score and rank outputs (the details vary depending on each task, though are easy to understand). The issue of DPO with tied instances is shown clearly in Figures 1-2 and compared against a filtering-based approach (both serve as natural baselines for their new loss). Modest improvements are obtained for both MT (Table 1) and math reasoning over DPO trained without tied instances (my main concern relatses to the narrowness of these tasks and the modesty of the improvements). Additional analysis is done (all in the appendix) to show that their approaches yield higher reward accuracy, enforce improved regularization (a technical argument for this is provided 3.3) and has improved convergence behavior (given limited time, I only had a cursory view of these results. I would encourage the authors to move the most important parts of this into the main text).  

==== 

Hansson, Sven Ove and Till Grüne-Yanoff, "Preferences", The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Winter 2024 Edition), Edward N. Zalta & Uri Nodelman (eds.), URL = <https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/win2024/entries/preferences/>.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
# strengths

-- A well motivated variant of DPO that directly addressed the important issue of ties in preference alignment. One that has clear and interesting connections with established variants of BT. I could imagine others in this area building on this novel direction and their approach. 

-- The paper is very well written and easy to follow. 

# weakensses 

-- (minor) The experiments are narrow, focusing only on MT, text summarization and math reasoning. I feel that these particular tasks are not sufficiently motivated and would like to see the authors address this. For example, it is not clear to me why MT is an interesting tasks wrt ties versus other tasks. 

-- The improvements reported in Tables 1-2 over standard DPO with filtering (as well as the baseline in row 1 for Table 1) are minimal and it is unclear whether they are significant. 

-- (minor) Their particular formulation in some ways makes the task more complicated by needing annotate tied data. It is unclear, outside of the tasks they chose, how such data can be reliably collected. I would like to see the authors address this, and also explain if they think that their approach could be extended so that this portion is learned.

QUESTIONS:
-- Was significance testing done Tables 1-2? 

-- Did you extend your approach to any additional tasks? 

-- Have you considered making the binary `t` terms in Eq 14 real-valued? I ask because I'm left wondering how your approach relates to recent attempts at interpolating between multiple losses, e.g., in RainbowPO [Zhao et al. ICLR 2025]

-- I'm also left wondering whether your approach could be easily extended to handle noise. Conceptually, ties seem to express a symmetry between the scores of the winner and loser, either they are equally "bad" (i.e., their probability should be driven towards zero) or equally "good" (i.e., their probability should be driven toward 1). From what I gather, your objective only accounts for the latter both not the former. Does this intuiton make sense?

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
While Bradley-Terry model assumes preferences have clear winners and losers, this is not the case in practice. In fact, previous work ignored data points that represent ties. This paper extends DPO to accommodate ties using two existing approaches: Rao&Kupper and Davidson. These models differ in how they assign probabilities to win and tie cases. They also introduce additional parameters that control the mass assigned to ties. Analyzing the gradients, these two variants (DPO-RK and DPO-D) exhibit different scaling factors in gradient estimation. Experimental results on NMT and summarization suggest that (i) adding ties have a regularization effect, (ii) DPO-RK and DPO-D achieves better KL vs performance curves, (iii) while DPO reward estimation is better for win/loss, other variants offer better reward estimation for ties.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
# Strengths
1. The paper is well-written and easy-to-follow. It provides a clear motivation on why ties could help and successfully points to existing work in utility theory for the proposed problem.

2. The authors introduce two extensions: DPO-RK and DPO-D. Both extensions are clearly explained and connected to existing baseline DPO model.

3. Experimental results are conducted on real-world benchmarks (NMT and summarization) with KL-performance curves and ablations on $\beta$ parameter.

# Weaknesses
1. **Lack of baselines.** There exists a large body of preference optimization objectives that add margins, different losses, etc. that are not studied in the paper. While the introduced variants show some promise, it is not clear if they are already captured by the existing DPO variants in the literature.

2. **Simple DPO baselines for ties.** Can you run experiments with baseline DPO where different weights are assigned to win/loss or ties? Is it that introduced variants capture ties better or can a simple extension of DPO also work well in practice?

3. **Clarification on TP dataset.** When you are creating the TP dataset, are you using only one direction for ties? I.e., given $y_1$ and $y_2$ is a tie, is only one of $y_1 > y_2$ or $y_1 < y_2$ present in the TP dataset for DPO? If it is the case, how would adding both directions impact DPO performance?

4. **DPO-x ablations.** Can you train DPO-RK and DPO-D on CP-only data? It is not clear if extending the dataset with ties or the slightly different preference modeling is why these variants work better.

5. **Clarification on Figure-3.** In Figure-3 (left), DPO achieves close to zero preference probability when $d_\theta$ is also close to zero. Does this mean that, when used as a reward model to rank candidates, DPO performs better for "almost ties"?

6. **Minor typos.** In Eq (23), $\beta$ should be removed from the RHS.

QUESTIONS:
1. Please add more preference learning baselines.

2. Please develop simple DPO baselines to incorporate ties.

3. Please clarify how TP dataset is created.

4. Please run more experiments with DPO-RK (CP) and DPO-D (CP).

5. Would DPO serve as a better reward modeling for near-ties based on Figure-3?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) effectively aligns large language models (LLMs) by maximizing the log-likelihood of preference samples, offering a simpler alternative to traditional reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines. However, the original DPO, which relies on the Bradley-Terry model, cannot handle "tie" outcomes common in real-world preference data, which are typically discarded, leading to information loss and performance degradation. To address this, this paper proposes two DPO extensions that support tie modeling: DPO-RK (based on the Rao-Kupper model) and DPO-D (based on the Davidson model), which explicitly model tie outcomes in pairwise comparisons. Experiments demonstrate that both methods significantly outperform the original DPO in translation, summarization, and mathematical reasoning tasks. Furthermore, the inclusion of tie data enhances model stability and generalization capability by providing stronger regularization.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
## Strengths
**Innovative and Effective DPO Extensions to Address Wasted Tie Preference Data**: Addressing the practical limitation of DPO's inability to directly handle "tie" preference data, the paper innovatively proposes two variants, DPO-RK and DPO-D. These variants explicitly model tie probabilities by integrating the Rao-Kupper and Davidson probabilistic models, respectively. Experiments demonstrate that these methods, when incorporating tie data, can prevent performance degradation and enhance model performance.
Thorough Validation of the Proposed Methods' Practical Value in Enhancing Model Performance and Utilizing Discarded Data: Through extensive experiments across multiple tasks such as machine translation, summarization, and mathematical reasoning, the paper demonstrates that DPO-RK and DPO-D can effectively utilize tie data previously discarded due to ambiguity or conflict. Consequently, they achieve superior performance compared to strong DPO baselines on these tasks and provide valuable strategies for handling and selecting tie data in practical applications.

## Weaknesses
**Neglect of β's Influence in α Parameter Sensitivity Analysis**: Although Appendix F.1 explores the sensitivity of α_RK and ν_D parameters, the experiments were conducted using a single β value (e.g., β=0.4 for DPO-RK and β=0.5 for DPO-D). The analysis does not investigate whether variations in β would affect the sensitivity results. Given that β is a core regularization strength parameter in the DPO framework, its value directly influences the model's learning from positive/negative preference samples. Therefore, when investigating the performance impact of α parameters (which control tie-handling strategies), neglecting β might obscure potential interaction effects between α and β. It could also lead to an incomplete understanding of the model's hyperparameter tuning mechanism.

**Inconsistent β Values Between Sensitivity and Main Experiments without Justification**: The β values used in the α parameter sensitivity experiments in Appendix F.1 are inconsistent with those used in the main experiments (e.g., Figures 1 and 2) where the model achieved optimal performance. No reasonable explanation for this choice is provided. This inconsistency raises questions: Why wasn't the sensitivity analysis of α parameters conducted at the β values that yielded optimal model performance? Could different choices of β alter the optimal values of α? The paper does not discuss this, which reduces confidence in the conclusions drawn from the sensitivity analysis.

**Ambiguous Definition of Hyperparameter α in Sensitivity Analysis**: In the main text, the paper explicitly substitutes the tie-control parameter ν_RK in DPO-RK with α_RK. However, it does not provide a similar substitution or equivalent expression for ν_D in DPO-D. Yet, in the sensitivity analysis in Appendix F.1, α is uniformly used to represent the tie-control parameter for both methods without necessary clarification or consistent symbolic linkage. This ambiguous definition and opaque symbol substitution can easily cause confusion.

QUESTIONS:
**Is Discarded "Tie" Data Necessarily High-Quality and Valuable Information?** The paper posits "tie data is typically discarded, leading to information waste" (e.g., Introduction, Lines 30-32) as one of its core motivations. However, the robustness of this premise is debatable: In real-world preference data, instances labeled as "ties," beyond genuine preference ambiguity, might also include data that is inherently low-quality, difficult to distinguish in terms of preference, or stems from annotation noise. Could the authors provide stronger evidence or argumentation to support the claim that the "tie" data repurposed by their methods generally possesses high informational value? This would be necessary to substantiate the universality and urgency of the "avoiding waste" motivation, rather than potentially introducing unnecessary complexity or interference in certain scenarios.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The author observed that the original DPO does not allow for ties for LLMs’ alignment, i.e., does not allow for ambiguity in the comparison of the paired examples in the training data. The author thereby investigate DPO variants that can incorporate ties during training. The authors proposed two tie-compatible DPO variants, DPO-RK and DPO-D, by replacing the BT preference model with the Rao-Kupper and the Davidson models.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths and weaknesses:
1. The motivations of the proposed method is clear: to allow ties for LLMs’ preference alignment during training.
2. The writing of the paper is easy to follow.
3. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of the proposed model.

Weaknesses of the paper:
1. The authors pair the top- and bottom-ranked responses as CPs and TPs as pairs that are close in the ranking. Not sure are there other way to construct CPs and TPs for training? Although the authors detailed the way they constructed the dataset for experiments, the constructed dataset was not publicly available, which making the reimplementation of the experiments impossible?
2. The paper suffers from the lack of novelty. The paper sounds like an incremental work the extension of existing Direct Preference Optimization, DPO. The main difference lies in the fact that the authors considered TPs in the objective.
3. Are there any other ways to accommodate ties in DPO? 
4. There are some DPO variants being proposed in the literature. Other DPO variants should be taken into account as baselines in the experiments.

QUESTIONS:
1. Are there any other ways to accommodate ties in DPO?
2. Besides TPs, are there any other factors that have impact on the performance of DPO for LLMs’ alignment? Any discussions?

LIMITATIONS:
The main limitation of the paper is that: this is an extension of the standard DPO model, which makes the contribution of the paper sounds marginal.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
(a) Summary: The paper addresses a significant limitation of the original Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) -- its inability to accommodate "tie" outcomes in training data, which leads to information loss because such data is typically discarded. The traditional DPO, relying on the Bradley-Terry model, assumes clear winners and losers, a premise often not met in real-world preferences. To overcome this, the authors propose two novel DPO extensions that explicitly model tie outcomes: DPO-RK (based on the Rao-Kupper model) and DPO-D (based on the Davidson model). These variants introduce additional parameters to control the probability assigned to ties.

(b) Strengths:
- The paper effectively highlights the practical problem of DPO's inability to handle ties, providing a strong and clear motivation for the proposed method.
- The paper introduces innovative DPO extensions (DPO-RK and DPO-D) by integrating established probabilistic models (Rao-Kupper and Davidson) to explicitly model tie probabilities. This is considered a more principled alternative than simply discarding tied data.
- Reviewers consistently praised the paper's clarity and readability, noting it is easy to follow and explains the extensions clearly.
- The paper conducts extensive experiments across multiple real-world tasks (machine translation, summarization, mathematical reasoning) to validate the practical value of the proposed methods. It demonstrates superior performance over strong DPO baselines and provides additional analysis on reward accuracy, regularization, and convergence.
- The work directly addresses an important, under-investigated issue in preference alignment, demonstrating clear and interesting connections with established utility theory.

(c) Weaknesses
- The constructed datasets used for experiments were not publicly available, raising concerns about reimplementability.
- Hyperparameter analysis issues:
- The paper's premise that discarded "tie" data necessarily possesses high informational value was debated, with questions about potential low-quality or noisy data included as ties.
- The formulation requires annotated tied data, and it was unclear how such data could be reliably collected outside the chosen tasks, or if this annotation portion could be learned.
- Questions were raised about whether a simpler extension of DPO (e.g., with different weights for ties) could also perform well, or if the benefit strictly came from the new modeling or the extended dataset.

(d) Reasons to accept 
- The paper tackles a fundamental practical limitation of DPO, which is the prevalent discarding of tie data in preference alignment. This problem is significant because ties are common in human-judged comparisons and represent valuable information.
- The introduction of DPO-RK and DPO-D provides a technically sound and novel approach to incorporate ties by building on established probabilistic models, demonstrating a deeper understanding of preference modeling beyond clear win/loss scenarios.
- Empirical demonstration of effectiveness across diverse tasks.

(e) Rebuttal:
- Reviewers questioned how tied pairs (TPs) were constructed and whether they held significant value. Authors explained four methods to construct TPs (e.g., using external metrics, conflicting annotations, or identifying pairs where both responses are correct). They provided empirical evidence that tied pairs contribute to a strong regularization effect and improved performance, supporting their utility, and committed to publishing the datasets.
- Concerns arose about the lack of novelty and the work being an incremental extension. Authors highlighted their contributions: the DPO-RK/D formulation, being the first to show the regularization effect of including ties in DPO, and proposing novel ways to model previously discarded data as ties. 
- Concerns were raised about the modesty of improvements and lack of significance testing. For Machine Translation, authors stated that a >+1.4 gain in KIWI-XXL is generally considered significant. For mathematical reasoning, they ran five additional random seeds, reporting median accuracy and standard deviation, which showed consistently higher median accuracy for tie-compatible variants, with gains generally greater than one standard deviation.
- Reviewers questioned the narrowness of tasks and MT's suitability. Authors explained MT is ideal for studying ties due to its natural variability and available automatic metrics. They also showed that vanilla DPO performs poorly at classifying held-out tied pairs, while DPO-RK/D models achieved higher overall classification accuracies for ties.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
